"There it is, sir," I said, pointing to the drawer, where it lay on the floor behind a table, still covered with a sheet.

But be warned, what you'll see will be so shocking, it could make even the devil doubt his disbelief."

"Sir," I replied, feigning a calmness I didn't feel, "you're speaking in riddles, and you can't be surprised if I find it hard to believe you.

But I've come too far in providing unexplained services to stop now before I see the end."

"That's good," my visitor responded.
"Lanyon, remember your professional vows: what happens next remains confidential.

And now, you, who've been so tied to narrow, materialistic views, you, who've denied the power of transcendental medicine, you, who've mocked your superiors—watch this!"

He lifted the glass to his lips and drank it all in one gulp.
A cry escaped him; he swayed, staggered, clung to the table, his eyes bulging, gasping for breath.

As I watched, I thought I saw a change—he seemed to inflate—his face turned suddenly black and his features seemed to melt and change—and the next moment, I had jumped to my feet and backed up against the wall, my arms raised to protect myself from this monstrosity, my mind drowning in fear.

"Oh God!" I screamed, and "Oh God!" again and again; because there in front of me—pale, shaking, barely conscious, and blindly reaching out with his hands, like a man brought back from the dead—stood Henry Jekyll!

originalSentences: What he told me in the next hour, I can't bring myself to write down.
I saw what I saw, I heard what I heard, and it made my soul sick; and yet now, when that sight has faded from my eyes, I ask myself if I believe it, and I can't answer.
My life has been turned upside down; I can't sleep; the deepest fear haunts me day and night; and I feel that my days are numbered, and that I must die; and yet I'll die not believing.
The evil that man revealed to me, even with tears of regret, is something I can't even think about without feeling horrified.

originalSentences: I'll say just one thing, Utterson, and if you can believe it, it will be more than enough.
The creature who sneaked into my house that night was, according to Jekyll's own admission, known as Hyde and was being hunted all over the country as Carew's murderer.
HASTIE LANYON.

HENRY JEKYLL’S FULL STATEMENT OF THE CASE

I was born in the year 18— into a wealthy family, blessed with great intelligence, naturally hardworking, and respected by the wise and good among my peers.
simplifiedSentences: I will tell you one thing. The man was Hyde. He killed someone. He was also Jekyll.

originalSentences: So, you might assume I was destined for a successful and distinguished future.
My worst fault was an impatient cheerfulness, which has brought happiness to many, but I found it hard to reconcile with my strong desire to appear serious and dignified in public.
As a result, I hid my pleasures and by the time I reached an age of self-reflection, I realized I was leading a double life.
Many men would have flaunted the kind of missteps I was guilty of, but because of the high standards I set for myself, I felt an intense sense of shame and kept them hidden.
simplifiedSentences: I was born rich. I was smart. I was happy. But I had a secret.

originalSentences: It was more my high aspirations than any particular faults that made me who I was, and created a deeper divide in me between good and evil, more so than in most men.
In this situation, I was forced to deeply contemplate the harsh realities of life, which form the basis of religion and are a major source of distress.
Despite my double-dealing, I was not a hypocrite; I was equally committed to both sides of my nature.
I was no less myself when I cast off restraint and indulged in shameful acts, than when I worked in broad daylight to advance knowledge or alleviate sorrow and suffering.
simplifiedSentences: I wanted to be good. But I did bad things. I hid them. I felt bad.

originalSentences: My scientific studies, which were focused on the mystical and transcendental, shed light on this internal conflict.
Every day, from both a moral and intellectual perspective, I drew closer to a truth that has led to my terrible downfall: that man is not truly one, but truly two.
I say two, because that's as far as my understanding goes.
Others will follow and surpass me on this path, and I predict that man will eventually be recognized as a complex mix of diverse, inconsistent, and independent elements.
simplifiedSentences: I studied a lot. I learned about good and bad. I was both. I was two people.

originalSentences: As for me, due to the nature of my life, I was inevitably moving in one direction and one direction only.
Remember to keep Stevenson's dark Victorian atmosphere while making the language accessible to modern readers.
I came to understand the deep and fundamental duality of man from a moral perspective and through my own experiences.
I realized that the two conflicting natures within my consciousness were both integral parts of me.
simplifiedSentences: I was two people. I wanted to be one. I wanted to be good. I wanted to be bad.

originalSentences: Even before my scientific research suggested the slightest possibility of such a miracle, I had begun to fantasize about separating these elements.
I thought that if each could exist in separate identities, life would be free from all its unbearable aspects.
The immoral could go on his way, free from the guilt and remorse of his more virtuous counterpart.
The moral could continue on his path of righteousness, doing good deeds and no longer vulnerable to disgrace and regret caused by this external evil.
simplifiedSentences: I wanted to be two people. The good could do good. The bad could do bad. They would not feel bad.

originalSentences: It seemed like a curse that these mismatched elements were tied together, constantly struggling within the tormented realm of consciousness.
How could they be separated?
I was deep in these thoughts when a new perspective started to illuminate the subject from my laboratory table.
I began to understand, more deeply than ever before, the fleeting, mist-like nature of our seemingly solid bodies.
simplifiedSentences: I did not like being two people. I wanted to be one. I thought about it a lot. I learned new things.

originalSentences: I discovered certain substances that could shake and peel back our physical form, like a wind blowing the curtains of a pavilion.
I won't delve too deeply into this scientific aspect of my confession for two reasons.
Firstly, I've learned that our burdens are forever tied to us, and any attempt to cast them off only brings them back with a more unfamiliar and terrifying intensity.
Secondly, as my story will unfortunately make clear, my discoveries were incomplete.
simplifiedSentences: I learned about our bodies. They can change. I won't tell you more. My story is scary.

originalSentences: It's enough to say that I not only identified my physical body from the aura and glow of certain aspects of my spirit, but I also managed to create a drug.
This drug could overthrow these dominant aspects and replace them with a second form that was just as natural to me, even though it was a manifestation of the darker elements of my soul.
I hesitated for a long time before I decided to test this theory.

I knew I was risking death, as any drug that could so powerfully manipulate the stronghold of identity could potentially destroy the very essence of who I was, especially if taken in the wrong dose or at the wrong time.
simplifiedSentences: I made a drug that could change me.
It could make a second me.
I was scared to try it.
I knew it could kill me.

originalSentences: But the allure of such a unique and profound discovery eventually overpowered my fears.
I had prepared my mixture a long time ago.
I immediately bought a large amount of a specific salt from a wholesale chemist, knowing from my experiments that it was the final ingredient needed.
Then, on one cursed night, I mixed the elements, watched them boil and smoke in the glass, and once the bubbling had subsided, I drank the potion with a surge of courage.

I made it a long time ago.
I bought a lot of salt for it.
One night, I drank it.

originalSentences: The most intense pains followed: a grinding sensation in my bones, severe nausea, and a spiritual horror that can't be matched at the moment of birth or death.
Then these torments quickly began to fade, and I felt like I was recovering from a serious illness.
There was something odd about my feelings, something indescribably new and, due to its novelty, incredibly sweet.

I felt younger, lighter, happier in body; inside, I was aware of a reckless euphoria, a stream of disordered sensual images racing through my mind, a release from all responsibilities, an unknown but not innocent freedom of the soul.
simplifiedSentences: I felt a lot of pain.
Then I started to feel better.
I felt new and happy.
I felt free and young.

originalSentences: I knew that with the first breath of this new life, I had become more wicked, ten times more wicked, enslaved to my original evil; and the thought, in that moment, thrilled and delighted me like wine.
I stretched out my hands, reveling in the freshness of these sensations; and in doing so, I suddenly realized that I had become shorter.
There was no mirror in my room at that time; the one that stands beside me as I write was brought in later specifically for these transformations.

The night, however, was well into the morning—the morning, as dark as it was, was nearly ready for the dawn—the people in my house were deep in sleep; and I decided, filled with hope and triumph, to venture in my new form as far as my bedroom.

I was happy about it.
I saw that I was smaller.
I went to my bedroom.

originalSentences: I crossed the yard, under the gaze of the constellations that, I could have sworn, looked down on me with wonder, the first creature of this kind that their unblinking watchfulness had yet revealed to them; I crept through the corridors, a stranger in my own house; and upon reaching my room, I saw for the first time the appearance of Edward Hyde.
I must speak from theory alone here, stating not what I know, but what I believe to be most likely.
The evil side of my nature, to which I had now transferred the defining power, was less strong and less developed than the good side that I had just overthrown.
Also, throughout my life, which had mostly been a life of effort, virtue, and self-control, it had been much less exercised and much less worn out.

I felt strange in my house.
I saw Edward Hyde in the mirror.
He was not as good as me.

originalSentences: And so, I believe, this is why Edward Hyde was so much smaller, slighter, and younger than Henry Jekyll.
Just as goodness shone on the face of one, evil was clearly and plainly written on the face of the other.
Evil, which I still believe to be the lethal side of man, had left on that body a mark of deformity and decay.
And yet when I looked at that ugly reflection in the mirror, I felt no disgust, but rather a surge of welcome.
simplifiedSentences: Edward Hyde was smaller than me.
He looked bad.
He was not nice.
But I liked him.

originalSentences: This, too, was me.
It seemed natural and human.
In my eyes, it was a more vivid representation of the spirit, it seemed more direct and singular, than the imperfect and divided face I had been used to calling mine.
And in that respect, I was undoubtedly right.
simplifiedSentences: He was also me.
He felt normal.
He was more real than me.
I was right about that.

originalSentences: I have noticed that when I took on the appearance of Edward Hyde, no one could approach me at first without a visible unease.

I believe this is because all people, as we encounter them, are a mix of good and evil.
But Edward Hyde, unique among humanity, was pure evil.

People are good and bad.
But Edward Hyde was only bad.
He was pure evil.

originalSentences: I only paused for a moment at the mirror.
The second and decisive experiment was still to be conducted; I had yet to find out if I had lost my identity beyond recovery and needed to flee from a house that was no longer mine before daylight.
Rushing back to my cabinet, I prepared and drank the potion again, endured the pains of transformation, and returned to being Henry Jekyll in character, stature, and appearance.
That night, I had reached a critical turning point.
simplifiedSentences: I looked at the mirror.
I had to try the drug again.
I drank it and became me again.
That night was important.

originalSentences: If I had approached my discovery with a more noble spirit, if I had risked the experiment while driven by generous or virtuous desires, everything could have been different.
Instead of emerging as a monster from these agonies of death and rebirth, I could have been an angel.
The drug didn't discriminate; it was neither evil nor divine.
It simply unlocked the prison of my personality, and what was inside rushed out.
simplifiedSentences: If I was good, things could be different.
But I was not good.
The drug made me free.
But I became Edward Hyde.

originalSentences: At that time, my virtue was dormant; my evil, fueled by ambition, was alert and quick to seize the opportunity.
The result was Edward Hyde.
So, even though I now had two personalities and two appearances, one was entirely evil, and the other was still the old Henry Jekyll, a complex mix that I had given up hope of reforming or improving.
The shift was entirely towards the worse.
simplifiedSentences: I had two sides now.
One was bad, one was me.
I was not good anymore.
I was getting worse.

originalSentences: Even then, I hadn't overcome my dislike for the dryness of a life devoted to study.

I still wanted to have fun at times, and since my pleasures were, at best, undignified, and I was not only well-known and respected but also getting older, this inconsistency in my life was becoming increasingly unwelcome.
It was this aspect that my new power exploited until I became its slave.
All I had to do was drink the potion, shed the body of the respected professor, and put on Edward Hyde like a thick coat.
simplifiedSentences: I still liked to have fun.
I could have fun as Edward Hyde.
I could be bad and no one would know.
I became a slave to the drug.

originalSentences: I found the idea amusing at the time and prepared with great care.
I bought and furnished that house in Soho, where Hyde was later traced by the police, and hired a housekeeper I knew to be silent and unscrupulous.
On the other hand, I informed my servants that a Mr.
Hyde (whom I described) was to have complete freedom and authority in my house in the square.
simplifiedSentences: I bought a house for Edward Hyde.
I hired a quiet housekeeper.
I told my workers about Mr. Hyde.
I made a will for him.

originalSentences: To avoid any mishaps, I even made myself a familiar figure in my second character.
I then drafted that will you objected to so much, so that if anything happened to Dr.
Jekyll, I could become Edward Hyde without any financial loss.
Thinking I was protected from all angles, I started to take advantage of the strange privileges of my position.
simplifiedSentences: I made Edward Hyde known.
I made a will for him.
I felt safe.
I started to use my new power.

originalSentences: People have hired hitmen to commit their crimes while they themselves and their reputation remained safe.
I was the first to do so for my own pleasures.
I was the first who could walk in public with a heavy load of respectability, and then, like a schoolboy, immediately shed these pretenses and dive headfirst into the sea of freedom.
But for me, in my impenetrable disguise, the safety was complete.
simplifiedSentences: People hire others to do bad things.
I did bad things myself.
I could be good and bad.
I was safe in my disguise.

originalSentences: Think about it—I didn't even exist!
All I had to do was slip into my laboratory, take a moment to mix and drink the potion I always had ready, and no matter what he had done, Edward Hyde would disappear like a breath on a mirror.
And there in his place, comfortably at home, working under the midnight lamp in his study, a man who could laugh off any suspicion, would be Henry Jekyll.
The pleasures I sought in my disguise were, as I've said, undignified; I wouldn't use a harsher term.
simplifiedSentences: I did not exist.
I could become Hyde and then disappear.
Then I could be me again.
I did bad things as Hyde.

originalSentences: But in the hands of Edward Hyde, they quickly began to become monstrous.
When I would return from these outings, I often found myself in awe of the depravity I had experienced vicariously.

This alter ego that I summoned from my own soul, and sent out alone to do as he pleased, was a being inherently evil and wicked; his every action and thought focused on himself; deriving pleasure with animalistic greed from any degree of torture to another; unyielding like a man made of stone.
Henry Jekyll was sometimes horrified by the actions of Edward Hyde; but the situation was beyond normal rules, and it subtly weakened the hold of my conscience.
simplifiedSentences: Edward Hyde did very bad things.
I was scared of what he did.
He was very bad.
But I did not feel bad.

originalSentences: After all, it was Hyde, and only Hyde, who was guilty.
Jekyll was no worse; he woke up again with his good qualities seemingly intact; he would even hurry, when possible, to undo the harm done by Hyde.
And so, his conscience rested.

As for the details of the disgrace I was complicit in (for even now I can barely admit that I committed it), I have no intention of discussing them; I only aim to highlight the warnings and the gradual steps with which my punishment approached.
simplifiedSentences: Only Hyde was bad.
I was not bad.
I tried to fix Hyde's mistakes.
I felt okay.

originalSentences: I had one incident which, since it had no consequences, I will only briefly mention.

An act of cruelty to a child provoked the anger of a passerby, who I recognized the other day as your relative; the doctor and the child's family joined him; there were moments when I feared for my life; and finally, to calm their justified anger, Edward Hyde had to bring them to the door and pay them with a check signed by Henry Jekyll.

But this risk was easily avoided in the future, by opening an account at another bank in the name of Edward Hyde himself; and when, by altering my own handwriting, I had provided my alter ego with a signature, I thought I was beyond the reach of fate.
About two months before the murder of Sir Danvers, I had been out on one of my adventures, returned home late, and woke up the next day in bed with somewhat strange feelings.
simplifiedSentences: I hurt a child.
People were angry at me.
I paid them with a check.
Then I opened a bank account for Hyde.

originalSentences: No matter how hard I looked around, it was useless.

Despite seeing the decent furniture and tall dimensions of my room in the square, recognizing the pattern of the bed curtains and the design of the mahogany frame, something kept insisting that I wasn't where I thought I was.
I hadn't woken up here, but in the small room in Soho where I usually slept as Edward Hyde.
I smiled to myself and, in my psychological way, began to lazily investigate the elements of this illusion, occasionally dozing off into a comfortable morning sleep.
simplifiedSentences: I woke up in a strange place.
I knew the room but it felt wrong.
I thought I was in another room.
I was not scared, I was sleepy.

originalSentences: I was still doing this when, during one of my more alert moments, my eyes landed on my hand.
Now, as you've often noted, the hand of Henry Jekyll was professional in shape and size; it was large, firm, white and attractive.
But the hand I now saw, clearly enough in the yellow light of a mid-London morning, half-closed on the bedclothes, was thin, veiny, knobby, of a dusky pallor and thickly covered with dark hair.
It was the hand of Edward Hyde.
simplifiedSentences: I looked at my hand.
It was not my hand.
It was Hyde's hand.
It was thin and dark.

originalSentences: I must have stared at it for almost half a minute, lost in the sheer stupidity of wonder, before terror suddenly jolted awake in my chest, as startling as the crash of cymbals.
Jumping out of my bed, I rushed to the mirror.
The sight that met my eyes turned my blood into something exquisitely thin and icy.
Yes, I had gone to bed as Henry Jekyll, but I had woken up as Edward Hyde.
simplifiedSentences: I was very scared.
I looked in the mirror.
I saw Hyde, not Jekyll.
I had changed in my sleep.

originalSentences: How could this be explained?
I asked myself; and then, with another jolt of terror—how could it be fixed?

It was well into the morning; the servants were up; all my drugs were in the cabinet—a long journey down two flights of stairs, through the back passage, across the open courtyard and through the anatomical theatre, from where I was standing, horrified.
I might be able to cover my face, but what good would that do when I couldn't hide the change in my height?
simplifiedSentences: How did this happen?
How can I change back?
The servants were awake.
I was shorter as Hyde.

originalSentences: Then, with an overwhelming sense of relief, I remembered that the servants were already used to the comings and goings of my alternate self.
I quickly dressed in clothes that fit me and made my way through the house, where Bradshaw stared and backed away at seeing Mr.
Hyde at such an hour and in such strange attire.
Ten minutes later, Dr.
simplifiedSentences: The servants knew Hyde.
I put on Hyde's clothes.
I walked through the house.
Bradshaw was scared of Hyde.

originalSentences: Jekyll had returned to his own form and was sitting down, with a troubled expression, pretending to eat breakfast.
My appetite was indeed small.
This inexplicable incident, this reversal of my previous experience, seemed to be spelling out my judgment, like the Babylonian finger on the wall.
I began to reflect more seriously than ever before on the implications and possibilities of my dual existence.
simplifiedSentences: I changed back to Jekyll.
I sat down for breakfast.
I was not hungry.
I thought about my two lives.

originalSentences: The part of me that I could project had been used and nourished a lot lately.
It seemed to me that Edward Hyde's body had grown, as if, when I was in that form, I felt a stronger flow of blood.
I started to worry that if this continued, my nature could be permanently tipped towards Hyde, I could lose the ability to change at will, and Hyde's character could become permanently mine.
The drug's power hadn't always been consistent.
simplifiedSentences: I felt Hyde was growing.
I was scared I could not change back.
The drug was not always the same.
I was worried.

originalSentences: Once, early in my experiments, it had completely failed me.
Since then, I had to double the dose on more than one occasion, and even triple it once, risking death.
These rare uncertainties were the only dark spots in my satisfaction.
But now, in light of the recent accident, I noticed that while initially it was hard to shed Jekyll's body, lately it had become increasingly difficult to let go of Hyde.
simplifiedSentences: The drug did not work once.
I had to take more.
I was scared of this.
It was hard to let go of Hyde.

originalSentences: It seemed that I was slowly losing my original, better self and becoming more and more like my second, worse self.
I felt I had to choose between the two.
Both of my natures shared memories, but all other abilities were unevenly divided between them.
Jekyll, who was a mix of both, would eagerly anticipate and enjoy Hyde's pleasures and adventures.
simplifiedSentences: I felt I was becoming Hyde.
I had to choose one.
Jekyll liked Hyde's life.
Hyde did not care about Jekyll.

originalSentences: But Hyde didn't care about Jekyll, only remembering him like a bandit remembers the cave where he hides from the law.
Jekyll had more than a father's concern, Hyde had more than a son's indifference.
To side with Jekyll meant giving up the desires I had secretly indulged and recently started to pamper.
To side with Hyde meant giving up countless interests and aspirations, and becoming instantly and forever despised and friendless.
simplifiedSentences: To choose Jekyll was hard.
To choose Hyde was to lose friends.
Jekyll would miss Hyde.
Hyde would not miss Jekyll.

originalSentences: The choice might seem unfair, but there was another factor: while Jekyll would painfully miss what he gave up, Hyde wouldn't even realize what he had lost.
As strange as my situation was, the terms of this debate are as old as humanity itself.
The same temptations and fears decide for any sinner wrestling with their conscience.
And like most people, I chose the better path but lacked the strength to stick to it.
simplifiedSentences: The choice was hard.
I chose Jekyll.
But I was weak.
I did not stick to it.

originalSentences: Yes, I chose to be the older, unhappy doctor, surrounded by friends and holding onto honest hopes.
I firmly said goodbye to the freedom, relative youth, light steps, impulsive actions, and secret pleasures that I had as Hyde.
I made this choice, perhaps with some hidden hesitation, because I didn't give up the house in Soho or destroy Hyde's clothes, which were still in my cabinet.
However, for two months, I stuck to my decision.
simplifiedSentences: I chose to be Jekyll.
I said goodbye to Hyde.
But I kept Hyde's clothes.
I was Jekyll for two months.

originalSentences: For two months, I lived a life of such strictness as I had never before achieved, and enjoyed the satisfaction of a clear conscience.
But eventually, time began to fade the intensity of my initial fear; my guilty conscience became routine; I started to feel a desperate longing, as if Hyde was struggling for freedom.
Finally, in a moment of moral weakness, I once again mixed and drank the transformative potion.
I don't think that when an alcoholic contemplates his addiction, he's truly affected by the dangers he faces due to his physical insensitivity, even one time out of five hundred.
simplifiedSentences: I lived a good life.
But I missed being Hyde.
I drank the drug again.
I did not think about the danger.

originalSentences: Similarly, despite having reflected on my situation for a long time, I hadn't fully accounted for the total moral numbness and readiness for evil that characterized Edward Hyde.
Yet, it was these traits that led to my punishment.
My inner demon had been caged for a long time, and when it was released, it came out roaring.
I was aware, even when I drank the potion, of a more unrestrained, more violent inclination towards evil.
simplifiedSentences: I did not think about Hyde's evil.
I did a bad thing.
I hurt someone for no reason.
I was like a bad child.

originalSentences: I guess it must have been this that stirred up the storm of impatience in my soul as I listened to the polite words of my unfortunate victim.
I swear, before God, no mentally stable man could have committed such a crime on such a pitiful provocation.
I acted no more reasonably than a sick child breaking a toy.
But I had willingly stripped myself of all the instincts that keep even the worst of us somewhat steady among temptations.
simplifiedSentences: I gave in to temptation.
I was very angry.
I hurt the man.
Then I was scared.

originalSentences: In my case, to be tempted even slightly meant to fall.
Instantly, a demonic spirit woke within me and raged.
With a surge of joy, I savagely attacked the defenseless body, finding pleasure in every blow.
It wasn't until I began to tire that I was suddenly, in the height of my madness, struck by a cold wave of terror.
simplifiedSentences: I ran away.
I destroyed my papers.
I planned more bad things.
Hyde was happy.

originalSentences: The fog cleared; I saw that my life was forfeit and ran from the scene of my crimes, both reveling in and trembling from my evil desires, my lust for life pushed to the extreme.
I ran to the house in Soho, and to be extra sure, destroyed my papers.
Then I set out through the lamplit streets, simultaneously reveling in my crime and planning future ones, while still rushing and listening for the footsteps of my pursuer.
Hyde was humming a song as he mixed the potion, and as he drank it, he toasted the dead man.
simplifiedSentences: I changed back to Jekyll.
I was very sorry.
I prayed to God.
I remembered my whole life.

originalSentences: The pain of transformation hadn't finished tearing through him before Henry Jekyll, with tears of gratitude and regret streaming down his face, fell to his knees and raised his clasped hands to God.
The veil of self-indulgence was torn from top to bottom.

I saw my life in its entirety: I traced it back to my childhood days, when I walked hand in hand with my father, through the self-sacrificing struggles of my professional life, to arrive again and again, with the same sense of unreality, at the damned horrors of the evening.
I felt like screaming; I tried with tears and prayers to suppress the flood of horrific images and sounds that my memory unleashed against me.
simplifiedSentences: I was happy to be Jekyll.
I locked Hyde's door.
I crushed the key.
Hyde had killed a good man.

originalSentences: Yet, even during my prayers, the ugly face of my sin stared into my soul.
As the sharpness of this regret began to fade, it was replaced by a sense of joy.
The dilemma of my behavior was resolved.
Hyde was no longer an option; whether I liked it or not, I was now limited to the better part of my existence; and oh, how I celebrated that thought!
simplifiedSentences: I was glad to be safe as Jekyll.
If Hyde came out, he would be killed.
I wanted to do good things.
I worked hard to help people.

originalSentences: With what eager humility I accepted again the limitations of a normal life!
With what genuine rejection I locked the door I had so often used, and crushed the key under my heel!
The next day, news came that the murder hadn't been ignored, that Hyde's guilt was obvious to everyone, and that the victim was a man highly respected in public.
It was not just a crime, it had been a tragic mistake.
simplifiedSentences: I was happy to be Jekyll.
I locked Hyde's door.
I crushed the key.
Hyde had killed a good man.

originalSentences: I think I was relieved to know this; I think I was glad to have my better instincts supported and protected by the fear of execution.
Jekyll was now my safe haven; if Hyde were to show himself even for a moment, everyone would be ready to capture and kill him.
I decided to make up for my past mistakes through my future actions; and I can honestly say that my decision resulted in some good.
You know yourself how hard I worked in the last months of the previous year to alleviate suffering; you know that a lot was done for others, and that the days passed peacefully, even happily for me.
simplifiedSentences: I was glad to be safe as Jekyll.
If Hyde came out, he would be killed.
I wanted to do good things.
I worked hard to help people.

originalSentences: I can't really say that I got tired of this helpful and innocent life; I think instead that I enjoyed it more each day; but I was still plagued by my conflicting desires; and as my initial remorse faded, the darker side of me, so long indulged, so recently restrained, started to crave freedom.

Not that I dreamed of bringing Hyde back; the mere thought of that would drive me mad: no, it was in my own person that I was once again tempted to mess with my conscience; and it was as an ordinary secret sinner that I finally succumbed to temptation.
Everything comes to an end; even the most generous measure is filled eventually; and this brief indulgence in my evil side finally destroyed my soul's balance.
And yet I wasn't scared; the fall seemed natural, like a return to the old days before I made my discovery.
simplifiedSentences: I liked my good life more each day.
But I still wanted to do bad things.
I did not want to be Hyde again.
But I did bad things and felt bad.

originalSentences: It was a beautiful, clear, January day, wet underfoot where the frost had melted, but cloudless above; and Regent’s Park was filled with winter birdsong and sweet with the smell of spring.
I sat in the sun on a bench; the animal inside me savoring the taste of memory; the spiritual side a bit sleepy, promising future repentance, but not yet ready to start.
After all, I thought, I was just like my neighbors; and then I smiled, comparing myself with other men, comparing my active kindness with their lazy cruelty.
And at the very moment of that self-congratulatory thought, a wave of sickness came over me, a horrible nausea and the most deadly shivering.
simplifiedSentences: It was a nice day in the park.
I sat on a bench in the sun.
I thought I was like other people.
Then I felt very sick.

originalSentences: These passed, leaving me weak; and then as the weakness faded, I began to notice a change in my thoughts, a growing boldness, a disregard for danger, a loosening of moral constraints.
I looked down; my clothes hung loosely on my shrunken body; the hand resting on my knee was veined and hairy.
I was Edward Hyde once again.
Just a moment ago, I had been safe, respected by all, wealthy, loved—with dinner waiting for me at home.
simplifiedSentences: I felt brave and did not care about danger.
I looked at my hand and it was hairy.
I was Hyde again.
Before, I was safe and loved.

originalSentences: Now, I was a hunted man, homeless, a known murderer, destined for the gallows.
My sanity wavered, but it didn't completely abandon me.
I've noticed more than once that in my Hyde persona, my senses seemed sharper and my spirits more resilient.
So, where Jekyll might have given up, Hyde rose to the challenge.
simplifiedSentences: Now, people wanted to catch me.
I was scared but I did not give up.
As Hyde, I felt strong.
I had to find a way to get my drugs.

originalSentences: My drugs were in one of my cabinets; how could I get to them?
That was the problem I was trying to solve, my hands clutching my temples.
I had locked the laboratory door.
If I tried to enter through the house, my own servants would turn me in.
simplifiedSentences: My drugs were in a room.
I had to find a way to get them.
The door was locked.
If I went in through the house, my workers would catch me.

originalSentences: I realized I needed someone else's help, and thought of Lanyon.
But how could I reach him?
How could I persuade him?
Assuming I could avoid capture in the streets, how could I get into his presence?
simplifiedSentences: I needed help from someone.
I thought of Lanyon.
But how could I talk to him?
How could I get to him without being caught?

originalSentences: And how could I, an unknown and unwelcome visitor, convince the famous doctor to search his colleague, Dr.
Jekyll's study?
Then I remembered that I could still write in my own handwriting; once I had that idea, the path I needed to take became clear.
So, I straightened my clothes as best I could, hailed a passing cab, and drove to a hotel on Portland Street, whose name I happened to remember.
simplifiedSentences: How could I make Lanyon help me?
Then I remembered I could still write like Jekyll.
So, I got a cab and went to a hotel.
I remembered the hotel's name.

originalSentences: At my appearance—which was indeed funny, despite the tragic circumstances—the driver couldn't hide his laughter.
I glared at him with such fury that his smile quickly faded.
Luckily for him, and even more so for me, because in another moment I would have surely attacked him.

At the hotel, as I walked in, I wore such a dark expression that the staff were visibly scared; they didn't dare exchange glances in my presence, but quickly followed my orders, led me to a private room, and brought me something to write with.
simplifiedSentences: The driver laughed at me.
I looked at him in a mean way.
He stopped laughing.
At the hotel, people were scared of me.

originalSentences: Hyde, fearing for his life, was new to me; shaking with uncontrollable anger, ready to kill, eager to cause pain.

Yet, he was clever; he controlled his rage with a great effort, wrote two important letters—one to Lanyon and one to Poole—and to ensure they were actually sent, he gave instructions for them to be registered.
From then on, he spent the whole day by the fire in the private room, nervously biting his nails.
There he dined, alone with his fears, the waiter trembling under his gaze.
simplifiedSentences: I was Hyde and I was scared.
I was also very angry.
But I wrote two letters.
I spent the day in a room at the hotel.

originalSentences: And when night fell, he got into a closed cab and was driven aimlessly around the city streets.
I say 'he'—I can't say 'I'.
This devil's spawn was not human; the only things alive in him were fear and hatred.

And when he finally thought the driver was getting suspicious, he got out of the cab and walked, dressed in his ill-fitting clothes, standing out among the night-time crowd, his base passions raging within him like a storm.
simplifiedSentences: At night, I got in a cab and drove around.
I was not human.
I was full of fear and hate.
When the driver got suspicious, I got out and walked.

originalSentences: He walked quickly, driven by his fears, muttering to himself, and taking the less traveled streets, counting the minutes until midnight.
Once, a woman approached him, I think offering a box of matches.
He hit her in the face, and she ran away.

When I came to my senses at Lanyon’s, the horror of my old friend might have affected me somewhat: I'm not sure; it was at least a small part compared to the disgust I felt looking back on those hours.
simplifiedSentences: I walked fast because I was scared.
I talked to myself and took small streets.
A woman came up to me.
I hit her and she ran away.

originalSentences: A change had come over me.
It was no longer the fear of the gallows, it was the horror of being Hyde that tormented me.
I took Lanyon’s condemnation partly in a daze; it was partly in a daze that I returned to my own house and went to bed.
I slept after the exhaustion of the day, with a deep and heavy sleep that not even the nightmares that haunted me could interrupt.
simplifiedSentences: I was scared of being Hyde.
I went home and went to bed.
I slept deeply.
I had bad dreams.

originalSentences: I woke up in the morning shaken, weakened, but refreshed.

I still hated and feared the thought of the beast that slept within me, and I hadn't, of course, forgotten the terrifying dangers of the day before; but I was once more at home, in my own house and near my drugs; and gratitude for my escape shone so brightly in my soul that it almost matched the brightness of hope.

I was leisurely walking across the courtyard after breakfast, enjoying the chill of the air, when I was seized again with those indescribable feelings that signaled the change; and I barely had time to reach the safety of my cabinet, before I was once again consumed and chilled by the passions of Hyde.
This time, it took a double dose to bring me back to myself; and sadly, six hours later, as I sat staring sadly into the fire, the pains returned, and the drug had to be administered again.
simplifiedSentences: I woke up feeling weak.
I was scared of the beast inside me.
But I was happy to be home.
Then I felt the change coming again.

originalSentences: In short, from that day forward, it seemed only by a great effort, like a physical workout, and only under the immediate effect of the drug, that I was able to maintain the appearance of Jekyll.
At all hours of the day and night, I would be hit with the warning shiver; above all, if I slept, or even dozed for a moment in my chair, it was always as Hyde that I woke up.

Under the stress of this constantly looming doom and the sleep deprivation to which I now subjected myself, even beyond what I thought possible for a man, I became, in my own person, a creature consumed and drained by fever, weak in both body and mind, and consumed by one thought: the horror of my other self.

But when I slept, or when the effect of the medicine wore off, I would almost instantly transition (as the pains of transformation became less noticeable each day) into a mind filled with terrifying images, a soul boiling with baseless hatreds, and a body that seemed not strong enough to contain the raging energies of life.
simplifiedSentences: I had to take a lot of the drug to be myself again.
Six hours later, I had to take more.
I was always scared of changing.
I was always tired and scared.

originalSentences: Hyde's powers seemed to have grown with Jekyll's weakness.
And certainly, the hatred that now divided them was equal on both sides.
With Jekyll, it was a matter of survival instinct.
CRITICAL: Maintain Stevenson's dark Victorian atmosphere while making the language accessible to modern readers.
simplifiedSentences: Hyde was getting stronger.
Jekyll and Hyde hated each other.
Jekyll was scared of Hyde.
Hyde was evil.

originalSentences: He had now fully recognized the monstrous nature of the creature that shared his consciousness and was destined to die with him.
Beyond these shared experiences, which were the most distressing part of his ordeal, he thought of Hyde, despite his vitality, as something not just evil, but lifeless.

This was the horrifying part; that the filth of the pit seemed to cry out and speak; that the formless dust acted out and sinned; that something dead and shapeless could take on the functions of life.

And this too, that this rebellious horror was bound to him more closely than a wife, more closely than an eye; it was trapped in his flesh, where he heard it whisper and felt it struggle to be born; and in his moments of weakness, and during his sleep, it overpowered him, and pushed him out of life.
simplifiedSentences: Jekyll knew Hyde was a monster.
Hyde was evil but not alive.
Hyde was a part of Jekyll.
Hyde took over when Jekyll was weak.

originalSentences: Hyde's hatred for Jekyll was of a different kind.

His fear of the gallows drove him to repeatedly kill himself temporarily, and return to his lesser role as a part rather than a person; but he hated this necessity, he hated the despair that Jekyll had now sunk into, and he resented the dislike with which he himself was viewed.

Hence the cruel pranks he would play on me, writing blasphemies in my own handwriting on the pages of my books, burning my letters and destroying my father's portrait; and indeed, if it weren't for his fear of death, he would have destroyed himself long ago just to drag me down with him.

But his will to live is incredible; I'll go further: I, who feel sick and cold at the mere thought of him, when I remember the depth and intensity of his attachment, and when I know how he fears my power to end him through suicide, I find it in my heart to feel sorry for him.
simplifiedSentences: Hyde hated Jekyll.
He had to kill himself to become Jekyll.
He hated this.
He wanted to live.

originalSentences: It's pointless, and time is running out for me, to extend this description; no one has ever suffered such torments, let that be enough; and yet even these, habit brought—not relief—but a certain numbness of soul, a certain acceptance of despair; and my punishment might have continued for years, if not for the final disaster that has now occurred, which has finally separated me from my own face and nature.
My supply of the salt, which hadn't been replenished since the first experiment, started to run low.
I sent out for a new supply and mixed the potion; the bubbling followed, and the first change of color, not the second; I drank it and it was ineffective.
You'll learn from Poole how I've had London searched; it was in vain; and I'm now convinced that my first supply was impure, and that it was this unknown impurity that gave the potion its power.
simplifiedSentences: I won't talk more about my pain.
I got used to my pain.
I could have lived like this for years.
But then I ran out of the drug.

originalSentences: About a week has passed, and I'm now finishing this statement under the influence of the last of the old powders.
This, then, is the last time, barring a miracle, that Henry Jekyll can think his own thoughts or see his own face (now so sadly changed!) in the mirror.
